FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, longitudinal cohort (N=543 at both year 2 and year 4 timepoints, ages 11-15 years)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis with linear mixed models examining VGA/SMA × time interactions on neural responses (reward/loss contrasts in 8 ROIs)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original research article published in Computers in Human Behavior (2025)

FT_IC2.evidence: VGA measured via 6-item addiction questionnaire; SMA measured via 6-item addiction questionnaire. Both treated as IVs in primary models predicting neural outcomes. Individual-trait-level outcomes: neural responses to rewards and losses during MID task (nucleus accumbens, insula, ACC, amygdala, etc.). VGA × time interactions on loss sensitivity in nucleus accumbens, insula, ACC (β=0.106-0.111, p=0.001-0.002)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results for VGA-neural associations: VGA × Time interaction coefficients (b=0.106, SE=0.034 for left nucleus accumbens loss contrast; b=0.111, SE=0.034 for right ACC), 95% CIs, p-values, FDR-corrected p-values; simple slope analyses; neural loss sensitivity × time predicting VGA (right amygdala b=0.086, right ACC b=0.088)

FT_IC4.evidence: Longitudinal design permits inference; multiple mixed-effects model results with interaction terms testing directionality of effects

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design confirmed, but SM (VGA/SMA) appears as both IV (Model 1, predicting neural response) and DV (Model 2, predicted by neural responses). Bidirectional analysis present; EC7 does not apply
notes: SMA showed no significant longitudinal associations with reward or loss sensitivity. VGA findings primary. Both variables measured via addictive-use scales, not screen-time-only. Distinction between VGA and SMA clearly maintained throughout analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study Release 5.1; cohort data from U.S. ABCD; cross-sectional networks at 2-year and 4-year follow-up (T2, ages 11-12: n=9,054; T4, ages 13-14: n=4,007); cross-lagged panel network (n=3,954)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using cross-sectional network analysis and cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) modeling; reports network centrality measures, out-strength, edge strength, partial correlations, and bidirectional associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed full-length report published in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2025)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen use measured as self-reported activity time (video games, social media, smartphones, TV, phone checking) and problematic screen use (functional impairment via Video Game Addiction Questionnaire, Social Media Addiction Questionnaire, Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire). Problematic use isolated as continuous functional impairment measure. Other side of relationships: psychopathology (anxiety/depression, withdrawn/depressed, somatic complaints, social problems, thought problems, attention problems, rule-breaking, aggressive behavior), behavioral tendencies (impulsiveness, reward/punishment sensitivity), environmental factors (family conflict, peer involvement, school participation). All individual-trait-level constructs. Problematic use exhibited high centrality in networks

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results: out-strength comparisons (e.g., problematic use vs. screen time in Figure 2C-D with 95% CIs), edge strength differences (partial correlations), centrality indices (strength, expected influence, bridge strength) with numerical point estimates and confidence intervals; cross-lagged coefficients in CLPN models

FT_IC4.evidence: Both cross-sectional network analysis (testing associations at single timepoints) and cross-lagged-panel-network analysis (bidirectional pathways T2 to T4) permit inference; numeric network statistics quantify associations

FT_EC7_reason: Design includes both cross-sectional networks (EC7 does not apply to cross-sectional) and cross-lagged-panel networks (bidirectional SM associations tested). Analyses are not SM-as-DV-only; both directions examined
notes: Paper emphasizes problematic screen use (functional impairment) over self-reported screen time. Problematic use showed greater centrality and stronger predictive effects on well-being than self-reported screen time alone. Network analyses isolate problematic use construct.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study participants; early adolescents from U.S. cohort (N=3,687 at year 2 follow-up, ages ~12 years; prospective analysis of year 3 mature screen usage)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis with structural equation modeling and multiple regression examining associations between sleep, sensation-seeking, and mature screen usage

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed original article published in Sleep journal (2025)

FT_IC2.evidence: Outcome variable is 'mature screen usage' operationalized as mature-rated video games and R-rated movies. Methods: 'they answered questions about mature screen usage' assessing mature-rated game and R-rated movie consumption frequency. This is a content-rating variable, not a phone/social-media modality measure. Criteria explicitly exclude: 'Mature-rated content / R-rated content variables alone (content rating ≠ phone/SM modality).' Predictor side is sleep and sensation-seeking (individual-trait-level), but the DV does not qualify as SM/phone use

notes: Paper investigates sleep and sensation-seeking as predictors of content rating (mature vs. R-rated) consumption rather than phone/social-media use per se. Does not meet FT-IC2 due to outcome construct being content-rating only
